speaker: interviewer
text: --was telling me about the day that you were being called for deportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I was desperate because all I could do-- I sent a message to [? Ruda. ?] We were supposed to leave. And, you know, you had 24 hours [? only. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we met, and he said he'd do everything to-- to be able to go with me. I didn't tell anything to my mother. I didn't know how he would arrange it. And somehow, I knew he would arrange it, deep in my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were terribly worried for my father, my mother and I, because he was still in jail in Prague. Last time when he was in jail, he was 80 days in solitary. So he survived it. But he-- you have to be exceptionally strong to do that with all the interviews-- if you want to call these interviews-- and if-- and the beating and the no food and the worries. He did. But what happened to him-- what's happening to him now, we had no idea. And don't forget that by this time, when he first went to jail in December of '39, so-- the factory was confiscated. And he lost everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there was-- it was just gone, what was in it, lock, stock, and barrel. So that was-- we knew that. But that somehow-- I don't know it-- but I don't really know, you know, if you ask me today, what-- where did the money come from that my mother bought-- was buying the food? She must have had money somewhere because the bank accounts were closed.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that was the first thing the Germans did. I forgot to tell you that. On the 15th of March, 1939, they matched in at 4 o'clock in the morning. By 8 o'clock, they were in Prague. And by 12 o'clock, all the Jewish accounts all over this part of Czechoslovakia that was occupied were closed. It was called-- they had a name. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means locked. And [NON-ENGLISH] is account. And they were all locked. So-- and they were not allowed to touch it. And so whether they were allowed to withdraw a certain amount or whether they-- they had money somewhere, don't ask me. You know, I often think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we live on? Where did the money come from? But it-- it must have been somewhere. At that point I didn't worry at all. I must say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, so we were sitting on this floor there. And-- and all of a sudden, [? Ruda ?] marched in accompanied by an SS man or two SS men. My mother was flabbergasted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- and [? Ruda ?] told me-- I said, how did you get in? And he said, I told them you were pregnant. My mother overheard this. Even on this floor, that was a-- she-- she yelled-- she managed to yell at me even there. Anyhow, what could she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said that our friends from [? El Al, ?] which was a Zionist organization, they-- and-- get to administration, the Jewish administration. And Terezin was in the hands of Zionists who were trying to save young people at the nucleus of future generations they hoped, because they knew exactly what was going on and where we are going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they promised him in Prague that they will somehow let the people know in Terezin that we are in this transport and they will try to get us out with my mother. And they did. We were sitting there on the floor in Terezin. I mean, you went with normal train to Terezin.

speaker: interviewee
text: You marched to the train station. And there were normal wagon, no cattle wagons and just normal third class benches. And-- and the trip took about an hour and a half, maybe two hours. But at that point, there was no rail going into Terezin. So it went only to a place before Terezin. And we had to march with our 50 kilos at least five, six kilometers into Terezin into this particular barracks where we sat on the floor for 24 hours. And we have given up hope because they said that the transport will reassemble and go back to the train station. And then, a friend of ours appeared on the scene. And they called our name and my mother. And we got up. We were the only people who got out of this transport. Transport disappeared, never to be heard of again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day did this happen on, Eva?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened-- you know, you always ask for dates. I have no idea. It-- it was in end of October 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and [? Ruda ?] and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother. And my mother was assinged-- she went into one of the huge barracks. There were four or five big barracks. And it was the Hamburg barrack that she was assigned to. And this-- this-- and [? Ruda ?] went to the Sudeten barrack. And then, I was assigned right away to this L309-- L309. The streets were-- you know, it was perpendicular always and at right angles. And the Germans gave the streets names, L, M, N, Q. If it wasn't the barrack, it was L. So  I was L309 was the name of the house we were. And they were the girls. And they welcomed me. And I have the-- I wrote out the names for you. Because if I spell it, we'll never finish. And I don't know. Maybe you want to attach it to it. But it doesn't matter. That-- what-- nobody's interested, not even my children, in the names of these girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you know these girls from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know them. I knew the two girls, the-- the twins, because I was in school with them. The other girls were from all over the country. But they were-- they were all in some Zionist youth movement. And we became great friends. Most of them are in Israel. And only three didn't survive from this whole group. The-- the twins, one is in Sweden. And one is in Scotland. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and of these girls, I think only one stayed in the Czech Republic. And the rest of them are in Israel. And two are in the States, I think. So that was not a bad percentage. But these girls, except for myself, all somehow worked-- they were assigned to work in the garden. There was a huge SS garden between the ramparts, where you grew vegetables for the SS and potatoes. And these girls-- the-- the Zionist ghetto elders, the ghettorat, they placed them in the garden because that was food. That meant fruit, you know. When I came, I was a little late than the others. There was no more room in the garden for me. And some of these girls, because they worked in the SS garden, they were protected from going east. So that's why most of them survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, they-- they-- and the ones that were not protected, they didn't make it except me. But most of them worked in the garden and never moved from Terezin at all, which was a lucky break. And they always had food. They brought tomatoes and potatoes and cucumbers or what have you. And that was bartering. So-- and [? Ruda ?] worked in the garden for a while too. We had-- we had potatoes and all of that. And then, my cousin-- I'm jumping, but it's part of the story. My-- one of my cousins, the sister of the cousin who is in England was a physician. And she worked in Prague. And she heard I was pregnant. And they send me parcels with diapers. My mother was climbing the roof. And she never believed me that I was not pregnant because they insisted that we get married. They-- they said they cannot justify it in front of the Germans if you don't get married because they got us out, because I was pregnant and got married. So we got married. And that was a stage. It was not a memorable ceremony, but it was there. The girls brought me from the garden three chrysanthemums, three white chrysanthemums. And somebody made us out of margarine and sugar and coffee sort of a cream. And we had a-- we had a celebration. And then, we had this wedding night on the-- in the attic where the millions of flies attacked [? Ruda, ?] and nothing. The-- the flies don't like me. Not flies, fleas. They don't like me. They don't touch me. And they all moved to him. So that was-- and the next day, I got scarlet fever. I got high temperature. And I had scarlet fever. And they moved me to-- they were very scared, the Germans, of any infectious diseases. And I was locked up for months in-- in a barrack for infectious diseases, where upon my mother decided I had an abortion. You never convinced her otherwise. You know, it was a set of circumstances. And you never, never convinced her otherwise. But she-- she lost her sense of humor or judgment after she was in jail. After she came back, she-- she-- she never became-- she never was herself. And then, they let me out. And then, it was-- I worked with-- with newborn children in Terezin, newborn babies because some girls came pregnant from other parts of the world, from Prague. And-- and they were-- and they gave birth. And these children, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was sad. They were like little rabbits. They-- the girls had no milk. And-- and we boiled everything. And we tried. Anyway, nobody survived, neither the mothers nor the children. And then, at Christmastime, [? Ruda's ?] parents came. And my father came straight from jail in his jail clothes. And the funny part of it is that my in-laws, they-- for them I was the long lost daughter. And it was all fine, even though I was-- I wasn't 16 yet. You know, my mother was desperate. I can-- I think, if [? my Mischa ?] would do this to me, I would be desperate too. I can sympathize with her. But those were desperate times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father just also thought it was absolutely, positively wonderful. Why not? He-- he-- he was very-- he was very upbeat about it. And for my mother-in-law, I was the daughter she lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even after the war, she was-- she would be mad at [? Ruda, ?] but I could do no wrong, whatever. Evicka-- you asked me what they called me. They didn't call me Eva. They called me Evicka. Evicka was a saint. You know, that-- you call people [INAUDIBLE] the diminutive. So they called me Evicka. Anyhow, so that was that. So we had enough to eat in Terezin. Basically, I mean, we were not overfed. But we were not starving. And we managed to feed the parents. We had potatoes and things and cucumbers and tomatoes. And we bartered. And-- and we were scared that they would send us east.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in September of 1942, '43-- I mean, we were there a year. So what did we do there? We-- we worked. And we tried to be alone as much as we could, which was difficult because Terezin was crowded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here and there, we went to a concert. They were always in the attic. Or there was a performance of one or two operas. They did mostly They did the opera that was produced for-- the children's opera for Theresienstadt, which was [INAUDIBLE]. And then, they had the poetry reading always in the attic. And the music was-- if there was a concert, it was wonderful because the musicians were from all over Europe. And they were all first class musicians. So that-- that was really uplifting. And the operas were also uplifting. I think they had-- did once or attempted to do something of that sort. I don't remember. Anyway, that was the life in Terezin. You-- you hustled for your daily bread. And you try to steal a few minutes alone. And-- and you tried to stay healthy if that was in your power, you know. And then came September 1943. And there was a transport going east, big one, 5,000. There was nothing for about six months. So it was a big lull and then all of a sudden, 5,000 people. And we were all in it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we moved heaven and hell to get out of it. You know, our parents and ourselves. We just didn't want to go in that. Nobody wanted to go. But we just moved heaven and hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we went to whoever we knew and tried to, you know-- what will help? Should we-- should we get sick? Should we, you know, give ourselves-- you can give yourself a [INAUDIBLE] injection. And you'll get high fever. They wouldn't transport even people to the gas chamber with-- who were-- who had a-- where there was a possibility of an infectious disease because they were afraid their own people will catch it. Anyway, through-- through this ghetto administration-- through this Council of the Elders, that's how-- it's the translation that they are using everywhere, altestenrat, we got out, all six of us. We managed, to-- again, through the Zionists, you know, we managed. That was September, and that was our incredible luck. And then in December, they called another transport, two, one on the 15th or 17th. And the other one went on the 20th, I guess. Again, two and a half thousand people. And we were again all  in it. And we were so tired from this September that we didn't-- we decided, [? Ruda ?] and I, we'll go. So they are all in it. We'll go. No. The parents were in it. We were not in it. And we decided we'll go. We'll volunteer with them. We won't let them go alone. The family should go together. We won't let them go alone once we are together. And we volunteered for that transport to go. And that was our luck. I don't know why we did it. I don't know why we fought so much, so terribly not-- to get out of the September transport. We did it. And that's why we survived. Because the September transport was-- they-- they established this family camp in Birkenau, B, Roman two, B. And then, we came at Christmastime. Nobody was gassed. Nobody was shaved. They-- they took our clothes away, our stuff away. But they left-- left us, I think, our shoes-- not-- not-- not shoes, no. They left us something, jackets. They didn't issue us any clothing. And-- and on March-- in March '44, the-- the people from the September transport, they were told they were going to be moved to a different camp because this camp is overcrowded. And they were going to work. And they gave them even a name they gave to this camp-- [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know. Anyhow, they-- they-- but the guys from the registry were Polish Jews and who had free access to all the camps, could walk back and forth. They came and said, this doesn't look good. This really doesn't look good. And there is a big-- they called the gassing [NON-ENGLISH]. The big action is being prepared. And they moved them from this BWB. If we want to look at the map next door, which was called quarantine camp just over the wire. You know, we all saw them there. You know. And then at night, at 7 o'clock, there was a-- something that's called [NON-ENGLISH]. They called a-- they-- you weren't allowed out of the barracks. They came with trucks and dogs. And they went, all 5,000 of them, that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went where? Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around the corner to the gas chamber--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: --all of them with dogs and SS and screaming and screeching. We all heard it. We all witnessed it. And-- and then, you know, the chimneys-- we called that you go to the chimney when you went to the gas chamber because they were-- the chimneys were huge. And then, they were burning, you know. So the-- the flames and-- went, and the-- that was-- they were shooting sky-high. The whole sky was red from that. And the-- the-- and you were covered with-- with-- with the ashes from that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the smoke. It was setting-- settling on you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the chimneys were working overtime. It's there the next day. But they-- they went-- they were-- they were gassed that night. The burning took longer, another day or two. And after that, we knew they were there for six months. And the-- the guys from the registry told us that they-- on our red cards, on our files, there is-- from the family camp, there is six months. Family camp then [NON-ENGLISH], which means [NON-ENGLISH], special treatment. Yeah? [NON-ENGLISH]. And that we have it too. So we knew that on June-- in June-- around June-- end of June, June 20, our day. June 20, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Was-- was-- was our date. So that's when they started with this uprising that they won't go voluntary. They won't go meekly like the-- the other 5,000 went who were completely surprised. They-- they didn't know that would happen. You know, they were women, men, children for the first time in the history of Auschwitz. And they were not shaved. And they had this children's barrack. So not that they got anything to eat or-- or more. But it was a different treatment.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after this march, nobody believed them. Nobody believed in anything. So people who had access and who had some-- smuggled in some weapons because they were working-- they were making in the satellite camps, weapons and gas and prepare some bombs. I wasn't really part of this at all. But I knew about it. Who was they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They young men.

speaker: interviewer
text: The young inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure. Who else? And-- and there was one thing-- interesting thing. In April of 1944, one man managed to escape from Auschwitz with the help of an SS, Romanian SS, who was in love with a girl from our group, a beautiful girl. She's in New York now, apparently. Very religious, I was told that she joined the Hasidim or-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- this-- this SS man was in love with the girl. And he wanted her to get out. And he-- he needed help. So he went to this guy we knew. And he offered to get him out in an SS uniform if he could-- if he would have access outside to some gold that he could return for the girl. They got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, [? Slava ?] was his name, he got to Terezin. And he told them what's going on. And they send the letters what's going on to Geneva, to the World Congress. And nobody did anything. They all-- all they had to do is bomb the access, bomb the railway. They couldn't rebuild it at that point in time anymore. And they would have had to stop. This SS man, incidentally, returned for the girl. And they caught him. And they didn't know about the girl. She got out. And they caught him, and he paid with his life.

speaker: interviewee
text: He got-- he got out of this-- anyway, so that is a vignette. And we-- so we were waiting for this June to happen. What will happen? And then in beginning of June-- I don't know. It was June. They said that they will have a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they went through a selection of the men.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they chose 1,500 men, and-- young men, strong men that they could-- will go and work in Germany. And nobody knew whether to believe that or not. You know, they went through a proper selection. My father didn't pass. [? Ruda's ?] father didn't pass. And [? Ruda ?] did. And then, they just-- they didn't know whether really to start anything because they, the guys from the registry who were their spies and who were their informers, they said there is no-- no action is being prepared. And they probably will leave. There is a normal train being readied. So they said they give it a chance. And they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva, we're going to take a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So let's take--
